frontmatter.title: Chapter 5 — Colour and the Imaging Pipeline

children.value: From Scene Light to Stored File

children.label: From Scene Light to Stored File

children.value: Every concept from Chapters 1–4 applied independently to each colour channel. This chapter adds colour, traces the full pipeline from photon to JPEG, and counts every transformation that corrupts the pixel value along the way.
A grayscale image has one value per pixel. A colour image has three — one per channel:

Grayscale:  I[i, j]          — 2D array, shape (H, W)
Colour:     I[i, j, c]       — 3D array, shape (H, W, 3),  c ∈ {R, G, B}
Each channel is an independent grayscale image. The perceived colour comes from the ratio of R, G, B intensities at each pixel.
All physics from Chapters 1–4 applies to each channel independently:
Shot noise is Poisson per channel

children.html: I2=aI1+bI_2 = aI_1 + bI2​=aI1​+b
a,ba, ba,b

children.value: Aliasing occurs independently along rows and columns in each channel
5.2 The Bayer Filter — How Sensors Capture Colour

children.label: 5.2 The Bayer Filter — How Sensors Capture Colour

children.value:
R  G  R  G  R  G
G  B  G  B  G  B
R  G  R  G  R  G
G  B  G  B  G  B
Each photosite records only one channel. The pattern has 2× more green photosites than red or blue — matching the human visual system’s higher sensitivity to green.
The missing two channels at each pixel are
— itself a form of reconstruction, and itself subject to error at sharp edges (colour fringing).
When colour is not needed, converting to grayscale with a
formula preserves the appearance of brightness:

children.html: L=0.2126⋅R+0.7152⋅G+0.0722⋅BL = 0.2126 \cdot R + 0.7152 \cdot G + 0.0722 \cdot BL=0.2126⋅R+0.7152⋅G+0.0722⋅B

children.value: This is the ITU-R BT.709 standard. The large green coefficient reflects the eye’s sensitivity peak. Simple averaging (

children.html: L=(R+G+B)/3L = (R+G+B)/3L=(R+G+B)/3

children.value: ) gives wrong perceptual brightness — a fully saturated red looks darker than it should.
For most shape, texture, and edge detection tasks, luminance is all you need. Colour adds value when distinguishing objects that differ in hue but not brightness.
Every digital image passes through this chain from scene to stored file:

Scene (continuous light)
  │
  ▼  [Lens] — focuses light onto sensor plane
Photosites (spatial sampling at grid Δx × Δy)
  │  + Shot noise added (Poisson, per photosite)
  │  + Dark current accumulates
  ▼
Bayer filter (one channel recorded per photosite)
  │
  ▼  [ADC] — voltage → integer  (quantization)
Raw image (one channel per photosite, 12–16 bit)
  │
  ▼  [ISP] — demosaicing, white balance, tone mapping, sharpening
RGB image (3 channels, 8–16 bit)
  │
  ▼  [Codec] — JPEG/PNG compression
Stored image (uint8, 3 channels)
Every arrow is a transformation that changes pixel values without changing the scene.
The final stored pixel value reflects:
Scene content — the signal we want

children.html: aaa
bbb

children.value: ISP choices (white balance, tone curve, sharpening)
Items 2–7 are nuisances for any pixel-comparison algorithm.

children.value: Every row in this table is a reason to distrust raw pixel values as direct measurements of scene content. Part II builds the case formally and develops the normalisation tools that address as many of these as possible.
